content.assessment: From a network-planning lens, status quo maintenance neither addresses nor worsens the geographic concentration of 6-/8-year tracks in Budapest and regional centers, nor the contracting feeder network of 1,200+ settlements without schools. Monitoring alone does not alter the structural network conditions (geographic concentration, contracting primary feeder base, rural teacher shortages) that produce and sustain segregation.
opportunities: Existing ABC/PISA data feed could in principle be repurposed for future network-capacity planning even if unused now
strengths: Requires no new network infrastructure or capacity investment, avoiding immediate transport or capacity strain
Preserves existing one-hour public transport access design without disruption
threats: Continued demographic contraction and rural teacher shortages could further erode feeder-network capacity absent intervention
Persistent geographic concentration likely deepens access disparities between urban and rural settlements over time
weaknesses: Does nothing to address geographic concentration of gimnázium tracks in Budapest and regional centers
Ignores the contracting feeder network (loss of 88 primary sites 2021-2024, 1,200+ settlements without institutions) from which selection cohorts are drawn
Monitoring infrastructure has historically not translated into corrective network policy
